verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: St. Chrysostom’s Commentary on the Epistle to the Romans is one of the closest and most argumentative of those he has left us.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: The style of the Epistle itself called for this, being such as almost constantly to remind an attentive reader of the necessity of forming some notion of the views and feelings of the persons to whom it was originally addressed.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: To this point St. Chrysostom has paid much attention, and has consequently obtained a far clearer view of the doctinal bearing of the Epistle than most other commentators.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: His early rhetorical education would probably have given him even too strong a bias toward that kind of exposition, but for his subsequent course of severe discipline and ascetic devotion.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: As it is, the rhetorical element in his commentary is of very great value.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: His ready apprehension of the effect intended to be produced by the style and wording of a sentence, is often the means of clearing up what might otherwise seem obscure or even inconsistent.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: An example of this occurs in the beginning of the seventh chapter, which he expounds in the 12th Homily.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: The illustration of our release from the Law of Moses by partaking in the Death of Christ, by the dissolution of marriage at death, is so stated in the Epistle as to contain an apparent inconsistency, as though the death of the Law, and the death of the person, were confounded.

verses.text: And the various readings only shift the difficulty, without removing it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: This, however, he has very ably shown to be, in fact, an argument a fortiori.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Other cases will strike other persons as they happen to have found difficulty in the Text.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: A far higher qualification for interpreting St. Paul, in whom, as much as in any of the sacred writers, the Man appears as well as the guiding Spirit, was that peculiar affection with which he regarded him, and which he expresses particularly in the beginning of the introduction, and at the close of the last Homily.

verses.text: The effect of this is perhaps best traced in the commentary on Rom. ix.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: The elaborate composition of these Homilies, and the close attention which it must have required, has been thought an indication that they must have been delivered before the Author was engaged in the cares of the Bishopric of Constantinople.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: But Tillemont has detected even surer indications, which place the point clearly beyond all question.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: In his exortation to Charity, Homily 8 he speaks of himself and his hearers as under one Bishop.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: It has been objected that he speaks of himself as Pastor, in Hom. xxix. but he does the same in other Homilies, certainly delivered by him when he was only a preacher at Antioch, and the terms are less definite than in the other case, v. ad.

verses.text: Besides, he seems to address persons who have ready access to the place in which St. Paul taught and was bound, which cannot be shown to tally with Constantinople, but evidently agrees with Antioch.

verses.text: The binding of St. Paul there mentioned is not, however, on record, and it is just possible he may mean in that expression to refer to another place.

verses.text: It may be worthwhile, however, to notice particularly, in connection with this work, the manner in which St. Chrysostom was quoted in the Pelagian controversy, as some of the passages were taken from it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: However, in Hom. lviii. in Gen. he says, “though he received help from above, yet he first did his own part.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: So let us persuade ourselves, that though we strive ever so much, we can do no good thing at all, except we are aided by help from above.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For as we can never do anything aright without that help, so unless we contribute our own share, we shall not be able to obtain help from above.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: This illustrates his meaning about doing our own part first, and shows that he does not mean to exclude Divine aid in the very beginning of good actions, only not superseding the motion of our will.

verses.text: The word gifts is also to be observed.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: He probably did not think of its being applicable to the first motions of prevenient grace, intending himself the Evangelical gifts.

verses.text: This view of his meaning seems to solve the difficulties of his expressions, so far as is necessary in a writer more rhetorical than logical.
